food_name: Grilled Chicken Chop with Vermicelli (Bun Ga Nuong)
aliases: Vietnamese Grilled Chicken with Rice Noodles

english: Vietnamese Grilled Chicken with Rice Noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese grilled chicken with vermicelli. FOODON_00002473 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (chicken meat, grilled) is used as the closest parent; the full dish includes rice vermicelli and condiments not captured by this code alone.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this composite Vietnamese grilled chicken vermicelli dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising a grilled chicken chop (approximately 150 g), a bed of rice vermicelli (approximately 150 g cooked), fresh herbs and vegetables (approximately 50 g), and nuoc cham dipping sauce (approximately 30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Bun Ga Nuong / Vietnamese Grilled Chicken Vermicelli — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05079); Rice noodles, cooked (NDB 20113); and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table values for fresh herbs and fish sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bun Ga Nuong exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted components: grilled chicken thigh (~39% of serving weight), cooked rice vermicelli (~40%), fresh herbs and vegetables (~13%), and nuoc cham sauce (~8%). Per-100g values are derived from the composite weighted average. USDA SR Legacy used for chicken and noodle components; FAO/INFOODS SEA used for herb and condiment components.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, white, boiled — GI value 53 (glucose reference); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 is assigned based on the GI of white rice noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Rice vermicelli is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The grilled chicken component contributes negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 51.3) / 100 ≈ 27.2, rounded to 27.3. Serving carbohydrate = 13.5 g/100g × 380 g = 51.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and nuoc cham dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 1178 mg sodium (310 mg/100g × 380 g), which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit or omit the dipping sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 53 (low) but a glycemic load of approximately 27 per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the rice vermicelli component and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and moderate saturated fat from grilled chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean grilled chicken with relatively low saturated fat (~3 g per serving), which is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from fish sauce warrants caution; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose reduced-sodium preparations and limit dipping sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Ga Nuong (Grilled Chicken Chop with Vermicelli) is a Vietnamese dish widely served in Singapore, particularly in Vietnamese restaurants. The dish consists of a marinated grilled chicken chop — typically thigh or breast meat marinated in lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, and chilli — served over a bed of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bun), accompanied by fresh herbs such as mint and perilla, bean sprouts, shredded lettuce, and a side of nuoc cham dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The chicken is characterised by a smoky, slightly charred exterior with a juicy, flavourful interior from the lemongrass-based marinade. The rice vermicelli provides a light, slightly chewy base. The overall flavour profile is savoury, herbaceous, and tangy. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, moderate-protein meal (~118 kcal/100g, ~10 g protein/100g) with a relatively low fat content (~3 g/100g). The primary nutritional concern is sodium, contributed by fish sauce and nuoc cham, making it a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 53 (low), driven by the rice vermicelli component, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~27) due to the portion size of noodles. This dish is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Vietnamese dining scene.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bun Ga Nuong exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted components using USDA SR Legacy (chicken, rice noodles) and FAO/INFOODS SEA (herbs, condiments). GI is assigned by analogy to white rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and restaurant-level variability in marinade and sauce quantities.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish type. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for grilled chicken and rice noodle components). Sodium estimate of 310 mg/100g reflects typical restaurant preparation with fish sauce marinade and nuoc cham; home or reduced-sodium preparations may differ significantly. The dish is classified under Singapore cuisine as it is served in a Singapore Vietnamese restaurant context, though its origin is Vietnamese. Halal status is assumed based on chicken and plant-based ingredients; individual restaurant certification should be verified. Price data from GrabFood Singapore (SGD 11.90) is preserved from the input record for reference only and is not used in nutritional calculations.
